points.text: Set a short connection timeout (e.g., 2‑3 s) and separate read/write timeouts to fail fast on slow dependencies.
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for retries and cap the maximum retry count.
Add a circuit‑breaker that opens after a latency or error threshold, halting further retries temporarily.
Make retries idempotent by using safe HTTP methods or attaching unique request IDs to avoid duplicate effects.
Collect TCP retransmission metrics (e.g., via netstat/ss or Prometheus) to detect packet loss during incidents.
Run latency, packet loss, and path checks (ping, traceroute, BPF) to identify network congestion or MTU issues.
Verify load‑balancer health‑check intervals and keep‑alive settings to prevent half‑open connections and timeouts.
Apply exponential backoff with jitter for retries to avoid thundering herd effects.
Set client timeout shorter than server's max processing time and propagate a deadline via context.
Introduce a circuit breaker that opens after a failure threshold to stop further retries.
Use bulkhead isolation to limit concurrent outbound calls and prevent resource exhaustion.
Tune TCP keepalive and retransmission timeout (RTO) settings to detect dead peers faster.
Instrument latency and queue length metrics on both client and server to spot bottlenecks.
Propagate request-level deadlines across service boundaries to keep overall latency bounded.
Profile code paths for blocking I/O or lock contention that may inflate service response time.
Check network for congestion, packet loss, and MTU mismatches that cause retransmissions.
Leverage connection pooling or HTTP/2 multiplexing to reduce handshake overhead and latency.
Set client timeout < upstream timeout to avoid cascading retries when downstream slows.
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for retries to spread load and prevent thundering herd.
Add a circuit‑breaker that opens after N failures and backs off for a cool‑down period.
Limit max concurrent retries per instance to bound request amplification.
Configure load balancer idle‑timeout and max‑queue‑size to drop excess requests early.
Enable TCP keepalive and tune net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time to detect dead peers faster.
Monitor kernel TCP retransmission counters (netstat -s, /proc/net/snmp) for spikes.
Tune net.ipv4.tcp_retries2 and tcp_retries1 to control how long TCP retries before giving up.
Check for packet loss or latency spikes with continuous ping/tracepath to the dependency.
Inspect load‑balancer health‑check intervals and timeouts to ensure they match service expectations.
Apply exponential backoff with random jitter to retry intervals to prevent retry storms during dependency slowdowns.
Implement a circuit breaker that opens after a configurable failure threshold to stop further retries until the dependency recovers.
Set client-side timeouts slightly above the 95th‑percentile RTT plus processing latency to avoid premature aborts.
Use connection pooling with keep‑alive sockets to reduce TCP handshake overhead and limit new SYN floods under load.
Monitor TCP retransmission counts, RTT variance, and duplicate ACKs to detect congestion or packet loss early.
Check for bufferbloat on network paths; high queueing delays can inflate RTT and trigger unnecessary timeouts.
Verify that ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) is enabled and that routers propagate congestion signals to TCP.
Inspect NIC and OS TCP settings (e.g., rto_min, rto_max, tcp_retries2) to ensure retransmission timers are appropriate for your latency.
Enable TCP keep‑alive probes on idle connections to detect silent failures without impacting active traffic.
Collect per‑dependency metrics (latency, error rates) and correlate with network loss events to isolate root causes.
Allocate a tail‑latency budget (e.g., p95) for each downstream call and subtract it from the overall request deadline.
Set the client timeout slightly above the sum of downstream budgets plus a safety margin (e.g., 10‑20% extra).
Limit retries to 1‑2 attempts and use exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering‑herd effects.
Implement a circuit breaker that opens after a configurable error rate, preventing further retries during an outage.
Use Little's Law (L = λ·W) to size request queues; ensure service concurrency does not exceed capacity under peak λ.
Monitor TCP RTT, loss, and retransmission counts; spikes indicate network congestion that can inflate effective latency.
Check for bufferbloat by measuring queueing delay on NICs and switches; high queuing can cause retransmissions and timeouts.
Validate that TCP window scaling and receive buffers are sized for expected bandwidth‑delay product to avoid unnecessary retransmits.
